AI continual learning is the ability of a model to keep learning from new data over time.
Continual learning helps AI systems adapt instead of staying fixed after initial training.
In continual learning, a model updates its knowledge as new tasks or examples arrive.
A key goal of AI continual learning is to learn new information without forgetting old knowledge.
Catastrophic forgetting happens when an AI loses earlier skills while learning something new.
Continual learning methods try to reduce catastrophic forgetting during ongoing training.
Unlike traditional training, continual learning does not assume all data is available at once.
AI continual learning is useful in changing environments where data patterns shift over time.
A continual learning system may receive data in sequences, streams, or separate tasks.
Some continual learning approaches store small samples from past data to review later.
Replay methods help a model remember older tasks by revisiting previous examples.
Regularization methods protect important model weights from changing too much.
Parameter isolation methods reserve different parts of a model for different tasks.
Continual learning can be task-based, domain-based, or class-incremental.
Task-incremental learning means the model learns a series of distinct tasks one after another.
Domain-incremental learning involves changes in input distribution while keeping the same general task.
Class-incremental learning requires the model to recognize new classes over time.
Class-incremental learning is often harder because the model must classify among all seen classes.
A continual learning model should balance stability and plasticity.
Stability means keeping useful old knowledge intact.
Plasticity means being flexible enough to learn new information.
The stability-plasticity dilemma is a central challenge in continual learning.
If a model is too stable, it may fail to learn new tasks well.
If a model is too plastic, it may quickly forget previous tasks.
Memory buffers are small storage spaces used to keep examples from earlier learning stages.
Rehearsal is the practice of retraining on old examples along with new ones.
Pseudo-rehearsal uses generated or synthetic samples instead of storing real past data.
Continual learning can reduce the need for expensive full retraining from scratch.
This learning style is important for robots, personal assistants, and adaptive recommendation systems.
An AI assistant that learns user preferences over time is an example of continual learning.
Evaluation in continual learning often measures both current performance and retained past performance.
Backward transfer describes how learning new tasks affects performance on older tasks.
Forward transfer describes how earlier learning helps the model learn future tasks faster.
Positive forward transfer means previous knowledge supports new learning.
Negative backward transfer means newer learning harms earlier abilities.
A good continual learner uses old knowledge to improve new task learning.
Continual learning is inspired in part by how humans and animals learn across life.
In practice, continual learning must often work with limited memory and computation.
Online continual learning updates the model as each example or small batch arrives.
Batch continual learning updates the model at intervals using chunks of new data.
Some methods detect which parameters are important and try to preserve them.
Elastic weight consolidation is a well-known regularization technique in continual learning.
Experience replay is one of the most common strategies for reducing forgetting.
Dynamic architectures can grow new model components when new tasks appear.
Continual learning research often compares accuracy, forgetting, and memory cost.
A challenge in continual learning is handling unclear boundaries between tasks.
Task-free continual learning studies how to learn continuously when task labels are unknown.
Concept drift occurs when the meaning or distribution of data changes over time.
Continual learning can help AI respond to concept drift without complete retraining.
The ideal continual learning system learns efficiently, remembers well, and scales over long periods.
AI continual learning is a foundation for building systems that improve throughout their lifetime.
